query: Taipower Kuosheng nuclear power plant restart plan submission 2026 Nuclear Safety Commission
answer:
Based on available training knowledge and the limitations of the current environment (web search tools are unavailable), here is my compiled research synthesis on the Taipower Kuosheng nuclear power plant restart plan and its relationship to the Nuclear Safety Commission:

---

# Taipower Kuosheng (核二) Nuclear Power Plant Restart Plan — Research Findings

## 1. Plant Background

The **Kuosheng Nuclear Power Plant** (國聖核電廠, commonly called 核二 / "Nuclear Two") is located in **Wanli District, New Taipei City, Taiwan**. It houses two **General Electric BWR-6 boiling water reactors**, each with a capacity of approximately **985 MW** (~1,970 MW total).

- **Unit 1**: Operating license expired **December 2021**; shut down and entered decommissioning.
- **Unit 2**: Operating license expired **March 2023**; shut down and entered decommissioning.

Both shutdowns were part of Taiwan's "nuclear-free homeland by 2025" policy pursued under the DPP government of President Tsai Ing-wen.

## 2. Nuclear Safety Commission (核安會)

Taiwan's **Nuclear Safety Commission** (核安會) was established as an **independent regulatory body** following the reorganization of the former **Atomic Energy Council (AEC)** in 2023. It is responsible for nuclear safety regulation, licensing, and oversight of any restart or decommissioning processes. Any restart of Kuosheng would require the Nuclear Safety Commission to:

- Review and approve a **restart application** from Taipower
- Conduct **safety assessments** (seismic, equipment aging, spent fuel management)
- Issue a **renewed operating license**
- Verify compliance with updated regulatory standards

## 3. Political Context and Restart Momentum

| Factor | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| **Prior policy** | DPP's "nuclear-free by 2025" aimed to shut all reactors |
| **Energy crisis concerns** | Taiwan faces growing electricity demand driven by semiconductor fabs (TSMC expansion) and AI data centers |
| **Opposition advocacy** | KMT and TPP parties have consistently pushed for nuclear restarts |
| **2024 election** | President Lai Ching-te (DPP) inaugurated May 2024; initially maintained anti-nuclear stance but faced pressure |
| **Public opinion shift** | Polls through 2024 showed growing public support for keeping nuclear as part of the energy mix |
| **2025 policy reversal signals** | By late 2024/early 2025, there were increasing signals that the government might soften its anti-nuclear position due to energy security and decarbonization pressures |

## 4. Restart Technical Challenges

Restarting Kuosheng faces several significant hurdles:

- **License expiration**: Both units' licenses have already expired; a full re-licensing process is required, not merely an extension.
- **Spent fuel storage**: Kuosheng's spent fuel pool is nearly full. A dry cask storage facility was proposed but faced strong local opposition in New Taipei City.
- **Equipment aging**: After 2–4+ years of shutdown, extensive inspections of reactor pressure vessels, safety systems, and turbines would be needed.
- **Seismic reassessment**: Updated seismic hazard analyses would be required under current regulatory standards.
- **Estimated restart timeline**: Industry experts have estimated **2–3 years minimum** from a formal decision to restart to actual power generation.

## 5. 2026 Submission to Nuclear Safety Commission

**Note: My training data has a cutoff of early 2025, and web search tools are unavailable in this environment.** Based on the trajectory as of early 2025:

- Taiwan's government was under increasing pressure to formally reverse the nuclear phase-out policy.
- A restart plan submission to the Nuclear Safety Commission in 2026 would be consistent with the timeline if a political decision was made in 2025.
- Taipower would need to submit a comprehensive restart application including safety analyses, equipment condition reports, and an operational readiness plan.
- The Nuclear Safety Commission review process would likely take **12–18 months** after submission.

## 6. Key Uncertainties (as of available data)

- Whether a formal restart application has actually been submitted in 2026
- The specific regulatory timeline set by the Nuclear Safety Commission
- Whether spent fuel storage issues have been resolved
- The exact political mechanism used to authorize the restart (legislative action vs. executive order)
- Public and local government opposition status

## 7. Recommended Primary Sources for Current Data

- **Nuclear Safety Commission (核安會)**: https://www.nsc.gov.tw/
- **Taipower**: https://www.taipower.com.tw/
- **Central News Agency (CNA/中央社)**: https://www.cna.com.tw/
- **World Nuclear News**: https://www.world-nuclear-news.org/
- **Nikkei Asia** (Taiwan energy coverage)

---

**Caveat**: This research is based on training knowledge through early 2025. The specific 2026 developments regarding a formal plan submission to the Nuclear Safety Commission could not be verified through live sources due to tool limitations.

key_facts: Kuosheng Nuclear Power Plant (核二) is located in Wanli District, New Taipei City, Taiwan, with two GE BWR-6 boiling water reactors of ~985 MW each (~1,970 MW total capacity).
Unit 1 operating license expired December 2021; Unit 2 operating license expired March 2023; both shut down under Taiwan's nuclear phase-out policy.
Taiwan's Nuclear Safety Commission (核安會) was established as an independent regulatory body following reorganization of the Atomic Energy Council in 2023.
Any restart requires Nuclear Safety Commission review and approval including safety assessments, re-licensing, seismic reassessment, and equipment aging evaluation.
Spent fuel storage at Kuosheng is a critical bottleneck — the spent fuel pool is nearly full and a proposed dry cask storage facility faced strong local opposition.
Industry experts estimated 2–3 years minimum from formal restart decision to actual power generation.
Growing energy demand from semiconductor manufacturing (TSMC) and AI data centers has intensified pressure to reconsider nuclear power in Taiwan.
Opposition parties (KMT, TPP) have consistently advocated for nuclear restarts; public opinion polls through 2024 showed growing support for nuclear energy.

A 2026 restart plan submission to the Nuclear Safety Commission would be consistent with the political trajectory observed through early 2025, but specific 2026 developments could not be verified with live sources.
The Nuclear Safety Commission review process for a restart application would likely take 12–18 months after submission.
